title: How to choose your first London vet
excerpt: Moving to London with a pet or getting your first furry companion? Here's exactly what to look for before you register.
body:
# How to choose your first London vet

Finding the right vet in London isn't just about geography — though proximity matters more than you might think when your cat decides to swallow something at 11pm on a Sunday. It's about finding a practice that aligns with your values, your budget, and your pet's needs.

## Start with location, but think beyond convenience

A vet five minutes away is wonderful until you discover they have a six-week wait for non-urgent appointments. Aim for a practice within 20 minutes, but cross-reference their booking lead times on Google reviews. Londoners in particular mention this constantly.

Also consider whether they have **out-of-hours cover**. Most GP-style practices refer you to a specialist emergency clinic after hours — and those can cost £150 just to walk through the door. Some practices, particularly the larger corporate ones like CVS and Medivet, have extended hours that soften this.

## Check their accreditation

The Royal College of Veterinary Surgeons (RCVS) runs the **Practice Standards Scheme**. You want a practice with at least a General Practice accreditation. Hospitals and Specialist accreditations indicate a higher level of equipment and capability. You can search the register at rcvs.org.uk.

## Independent vs corporate

This is genuinely controversial in the London pet owner community. Independent vets often provide a more personal relationship — the same vet knows your dog's history. Corporate chains (CVS, IVC Evidensia, Medivet, Vets4Pets) tend to have better equipment, more predictable pricing, and easier online booking.

The honest answer: both can be excellent, both can be poor. The vet you build a relationship with matters more than who signs their payslip.

## Ask these questions before registering

1. **What are your appointment wait times for non-urgent issues?** Anything over two weeks is a red flag.
2. **Who covers out-of-hours?** Get the name and address of the emergency clinic they use.
3. **Do you do free initial consultations?** Some do. It's a low-cost way to get a feel for the practice.
4. **What's your approach to preventative care — are you proactive about annual health checks beyond vaccination boosters?**

## The first visit checklist

Bring your pet's vaccination records, any previous vet notes, and a list of any medications or supplements they're on. Even if this is their first vet visit, write down the dates of any health concerns you've noticed, however minor. Vets are pattern-readers — the more context you give, the better.

Finally: trust your gut. If the receptionist is rude, the waiting room smells strongly of stress-urine and isn't being cleaned, or the vet seems rushed and dismissive of your questions — those are signals. Finding a vet you trust is worth a second appointment elsewhere.

## Petovia makes this easier

Our marketplace shows you verified London vets with real review counts, opening hours, services offered, and distance from your postcode. Filter by specialty, sort by rating, and read what other pet parents in your area have experienced. It takes the guesswork out of what is, frankly, an important decision.

title: What pet insurance actually covers (and what it doesn't)
excerpt: We compared 11 UK pet insurance policies so you don't have to. The gaps in cover will surprise you.
body:
# What pet insurance actually covers (and what it doesn't)

The UK pet insurance market has exploded — there are now over 60 providers offering policies from £5 to £100+ a month. The marketing language is warm and reassuring. The exclusions list is where the real story lives.

## The four tiers of cover

**Accident only** — the cheapest tier (from £5/month for a cat). Covers treatment following accidents. Crucially: does not cover illness. If your dog develops a cruciate ligament problem, that's an illness, not an accident, even though it manifests suddenly.

**Time-limited** — covers both accident and illness, but each condition is only covered for 12 months from first treatment. After that, it's excluded even if the condition is ongoing. This is where many owners get caught out with chronic conditions like arthritis or allergies.

**Maximum benefit** — covers each condition up to a set amount (e.g., £2,000 per condition) with no time limit. Better for chronic conditions, but once the benefit is exhausted, the condition is excluded.

**Lifetime** — the gold standard. The annual limit resets each year. A dog diagnosed with diabetes at age 4 will still be covered at age 12, as long as you maintain the policy without breaks.

## What's almost never covered

- **Pre-existing conditions** — anything your pet was treated for (or showed symptoms of) before the policy started, or during a waiting period (usually 14 days). This includes conditions your vet noted in passing even if they weren't formally diagnosed.
- **Dental disease** — most policies exclude periodontal disease and treatment. Dental accidents (broken tooth from a trauma) are usually covered; routine dental cleaning is never covered.
- **Preventative treatments** — vaccinations, flea/tick/worming treatments, neutering, microchipping. These are your responsibility.
- **Pregnancy and whelping**
- **Behavioural problems** — unless caused by a diagnosed medical condition.

## The waiting period trap

Almost all UK policies have a 14-day waiting period for illness (sometimes longer for specific conditions like orthopaedic issues — Petplan waits 14 days for general illness but up to 5 days for accidental injuries). If your pet shows symptoms during this window, the condition is treated as pre-existing.

## What to actually look for

1. Lifetime cover if your budget allows — it's the only tier that truly protects against the conditions most likely to become expensive.
2. A per-condition limit of at least £4,000–£6,000 (London specialist referral costs are high).
3. Does the policy cover **specialist referral**? Not all do, or they apply sub-limits.
4. **Dental cover beyond accidents** — rare but some policies (e.g., Bought By Many's premium tier) include it.
5. Check the **co-payment** (excess) structure — a 20% co-payment on top of a £250 excess on a £3,000 claim means you're paying £850 yourself.

## The honest take

For young, healthy pets: a basic lifetime policy (£25–45/month) bought the week you bring them home, before any health history accumulates, is almost always worth it. For older pets being insured for the first time: read the exclusions extremely carefully, and consider whether self-insuring (saving equivalent premiums into a dedicated account) makes more financial sense given the likely exclusion list.

title: Microchipping is now law — here's what to do
excerpt: As of June 2024, all cats in England must be microchipped. Here's what the law requires, what it costs, and how to check if your pet is compliant.
body:
# Microchipping is now law — here's what to do

From 10 June 2024, all domestic cats in England must be microchipped and registered on a government-approved database. This extends the existing legislation that made microchipping compulsory for dogs in 2016.

## What does the law require?

All cats over 20 weeks of age must be:
- Microchipped with a 15-digit ISO-compliant chip
- Registered on one of the UK's government-approved databases (Petlog, Microchip Central, PetDatabase, etc.)
- The keeper's details must be kept up to date

If your cat is found without a microchip, a notice is issued by an authorised person (local authority officer, vet, or police officer). You have 21 days to chip them. Failure to comply carries a £500 fine.

## How much does it cost?

Most UK vets charge between £20–40 for microchipping. Some councils and charities run periodic free or subsidised microchipping events. The Dogs Trust and Cats Protection regularly run free microchipping days — worth following them on social media if cost is a concern.

## Does your dog need one too?

Yes — dogs have been required to be microchipped since April 2016. Puppies must be chipped before they're 8 weeks old (before they can legally be sold or transferred).

## How to check if your pet is already chipped

Your vet can scan for a microchip at any appointment — it takes about 10 seconds. If you know your chip number, you can search it on the free service at check-a-chip.co.uk to see which database it's registered on.

## The database problem

This is where many owners fall down. Being chipped doesn't mean being registered — the chip itself contains only a number. That number must be linked to your current contact details on an approved database. If you've moved house, changed your phone number, or rehomed the pet, the register must be updated.

Some database providers charge an annual fee; others are free for life. If your pet is registered on a fee-based database and you've stopped paying, your record may have lapsed. Check now.

## What to do if you've just adopted a rescue

Rescue organisations are required to chip and register animals before rehoming. However, when you adopt:
1. Ask for the microchip number in writing
2. Within 7 days of adoption, update the registered keeper details to your own name and address — the rescue should either transfer it for you or provide a transfer code
3. Verify the update has processed (search the chip number to confirm it shows your details)

## In summary

If you have a cat in England and aren't sure whether they're chipped: book a quick vet appointment to check. It takes minutes, costs very little if any, and carries a statutory obligation. Use Petovia's marketplace to find a vet near you who offers microchipping — filter by the Microchipping service tag.

title: 5 signs your dog needs a groomer this week
excerpt: It's not just about looking good. Neglected grooming leads to real health problems. Here are the signs you shouldn't ignore.
body:
# 5 signs your dog needs a groomer this week

Grooming often gets treated as an aesthetic choice — something you do for photos and park walks. In reality, coat maintenance is a health issue. Here are the five signs that mean it's past time to book.

## 1. You can feel matting

Run your fingers through your dog's coat and press gently near the skin. If you feel dense, tangled clumps that don't separate, those are mats — and they're a problem beyond discomfort. Mats trap moisture, restrict circulation, hide skin infections, and harbour parasites. On long-coated breeds like Cockapoos, Cavapoos, and Maltese, they can form in days in wet London weather.

Do not attempt to cut out severe mats at home. A professional groomer will either work them out carefully or perform a 'strip' (shave down), which is uncomfortable but necessary. Attempting to cut near skin without proper training routinely results in nicks.

## 2. You can hear their nails on hard floors

Nails that click on your kitchen floor are too long. Overgrown nails change the way a dog loads weight through their feet and legs — over time, this contributes to joint problems and postural changes. Dew claws (the inner nail higher on the leg) can curl and grow into the skin if left entirely uncut.

Nail trimming is one of the most stress-inducing grooming activities for dogs with no previous positive association. A professional groomer trained in low-stress handling will manage this better than most owners can at home.

## 3. Their ears smell or they're scratching them

Floppy-eared breeds — Spaniels, Basset Hounds, Dachshunds — trap moisture and debris in the ear canal. An earthy or yeasty smell combined with frequent head-shaking or paw-scratching at ears usually indicates an infection brewing, often compounded by hair growing inside the ear canal. Groomers trained in ear cleaning and plucking can address this; a vet visit may be needed if infection is already established.

## 4. You can't see their eyes clearly

For Shih Tzus, Bichons, Miniature Schnauzers, and similar breeds, the fringe grows quickly and impedes vision. Beyond the obvious welfare concern, the hair itself carries bacteria into the eyes and contributes to tear-staining and corneal irritation. A trim around the face every 4–6 weeks is genuinely health-critical for these breeds.

## 5. They smell even after a bath

If your dog smells bad again within a day or two of bathing, there are a few culprits: anal gland impaction (one of the most common and undiagnosed causes of 'dog smell'), a skin infection hiding under a neglected coat, or — on double-coated breeds — a coat that didn't dry properly to the skin and has developed a mildew-like smell. A professional bath with proper blow-drying and an anal gland check from a groomer will address the first two.

## Finding a groomer in London

Petovia's grooming category is coming soon. In the meantime, our community has recommendations in the comments below — drop your borough and we'll point you in the right direction.

title: Travelling to the EU with your pet post-Brexit
excerpt: The old EU Pet Passport no longer works. Here's exactly what paperwork you need, the timelines, and what happens at the border.
body:
# Travelling to the EU with your pet post-Brexit

Post-Brexit travel with pets from the UK to the EU (and back) has become significantly more complicated. If you're planning to take your dog, cat, or ferret to the continent in 2026, here's what you need to know — and crucially, what needs to be sorted weeks in advance.

## The old EU Pet Passport is dead (for new journeys)

Pre-Brexit UK EU Pet Passports are no longer accepted as the sole travel document. Instead, you need an **AHC — Animal Health Certificate** — for each journey. There are some exceptions (passports issued by Gibraltar and Northern Ireland may still apply; passports issued when the UK was in the EU may still be valid for some pet categories in some countries — but don't rely on this without checking with your destination country's embassy).

## What you need: the AHC

An Animal Health Certificate must be:
- Issued by a government-authorised vet (an **Official Veterinarian** or OV) — not all vets are OVs; check the APHA list at gov.uk
- Issued **no more than 10 days before travel**
- In the official format for your destination country (most EU states accept the same template; some have specific requirements)

The AHC is valid for:
- **10 days** for travel into the EU
- **4 months** for onward travel within the EU (once you're in)
- Re-entry back to the UK within 4 months (as long as you haven't left the EU/listed country zone during that time)

## Your pet must be microchipped

The chip must be ISO 11784/11785 compliant (virtually all UK chips are, but older chips should be checked). This is verified at the border.

## Rabies vaccination requirements

Your pet must have a valid rabies vaccination. The rules vary by destination country, but the basic UK requirement is:
- First rabies vaccination must have been given at least 21 days before travel
- Boosters must be kept current (typically every 1–3 years depending on the vaccine)

If your pet's rabies vaccination has lapsed, the 21-day clock resets after the booster.

## Returning to the UK

You do not need a new AHC to re-enter the UK (the UK doesn't impose a pet passport requirement on re-entry from listed countries). However, your pet must:
- Be microchipped
- Have been treated against tapeworm (Echinococcus) by an OV vet 1–5 days before re-entering the UK (not required when entering most EU countries, but required when coming back to the UK for dogs — not cats)

## The checklist

1. Microchip — check ISO compliance ✓
2. Up-to-date rabies vaccination ✓
3. Book an Official Veterinarian appointment no earlier than 10 days before departure ✓
4. Check specific entry requirements for your destination country ✓
5. For return: book tapeworm treatment 1–5 days before re-entering UK ✓
6. Carry: AHC (original, not a copy), vaccination records ✓

## Budget for this

An OV consultation plus AHC typically costs £150–250 in London. Rabies vaccination (if needed) is £45–80. For a dog travelling annually, this is a meaningful cost to factor in.

title: Raw vs kibble: a UK vet's honest take
excerpt: The raw feeding debate gets loud online. We asked a South London vet to give us the unsexy, evidence-based answer.
body:
# Raw vs kibble: a UK vet's honest take

Few topics in the pet owner community generate more heat than raw feeding. On one side: enthusiasts who swear their dogs' coats transformed overnight, digestive issues resolved, energy improved. On the other: vets citing zoonotic risk studies and nutritional gaps. I've been a vet in South London for eleven years and I try to be honest about what the evidence actually says.

## What the research actually shows

The honest position is that the evidence base for raw feeding is **genuinely limited** — not because it's harmful, but because large, controlled studies on commercial raw foods are expensive and rare. What we do have:

- **A 2019 Utrecht study** found that raw meat-based diets carried significantly higher bacterial contamination (Salmonella, E. coli, Listeria) than heat-processed foods. The risk is primarily to humans handling the food, particularly immunocompromised people and young children.
- **FEDIAF nutritional guidelines** (the standard for complete pet food in Europe) are met by well-formulated raw diets, but not all commercial raw foods are formulated to these standards. Home-prepared raw without supplementation frequently isn't.
- **There is no robust clinical evidence** that raw feeding reduces cancer rates, improves longevity, or resolves most of the conditions owners commonly attribute to diet changes. Some of those improvements are real; many are owner observation bias combined with the placebo effect of care and attention.

## What kibble actually is

Commercial kibble ranges from nutritionally excellent to genuinely poor. The AAFCO/FEDIAF labels mean a product meets minimum standards, but the ingredient quality, digestibility, and processing methods vary enormously. A premium grain-inclusive kibble from a brand that employs veterinary nutritionists (Royal Canin, Hill's, Purina Pro Plan) is very different from a supermarket own-brand with vague 'meat and animal derivatives' listings.

## My actual position

If a client comes to me with a dog doing well on a balanced raw diet from a reputable brand (such as Natures Menu, Nutriment, or similar), I don't recommend switching. The anxiety isn't worth it, and I can't point to clear evidence the change would benefit the animal.

If someone asks me to recommend a diet from scratch, I recommend a complete dry or wet food from a company that employs full-time veterinary nutritionists and has feeding trials behind their products. The WSAVA guidelines list what to look for on their website.

If someone is feeding raw and lives with young children, elderly relatives, or anyone immunocompromised — the bacterial risk conversation is non-negotiable. Good hygiene practices (separate prep surfaces, handwashing, freezing protocols) are essential, not optional.

## The middle ground

Many owners do well with a mixed approach — a high-quality complete kibble as the base (which handles the nutritional balance) with raw toppers or whole raw meaty bones for dental and enrichment benefits. This is pragmatic and low-risk.

The thing I'd push back hardest on is the certainty in either camp. Raw feeding isn't going to poison your dog or cure their cancer. Good-quality kibble isn't going to shorten their life. Work with a vet who takes nutrition seriously, not one who dismisses your questions.

title: Crate training a rescue: a 7-day plan
excerpt: Rescue dogs often come with crate anxiety or unknown history. This gentle protocol works even for the most reluctant dogs.
body:
# Crate training a rescue: a 7-day plan

Crate training a rescue dog is different from training a puppy. You don't know what associations the crate might trigger. A dog that spent time in a shelter may associate enclosed spaces with stress, separation, or punishment. This plan assumes nothing — it starts from zero and builds slowly.

## What you need

- A wire crate large enough for the dog to stand, turn, and lie stretched out (cover three sides with a blanket to make it den-like)
- Very high-value treats — real meat, cheese, or your dog's highest-reward food
- Patience — this is a week-long plan designed around low pressure and success

## Day 1–2: Introduction without pressure

Leave the crate in the room with the door open and removed (or tied open). Put a treat trail leading to the crate. Don't interact — just let the dog investigate at their own pace. Reinforce every interaction with calm praise and a treat thrown near the crate. No prompting to go in.

Goal: dog is comfortable being near the crate without stress.

## Day 3: Going in voluntarily

Start tossing high-value treats into the back of the crate. When the dog goes in to get them, say "yes" calmly and give two more treats while they're inside. Let them leave immediately — no closing the door. Repeat 10–15 times in a session, twice a day.

Goal: dog is choosing to enter the crate voluntarily.

## Day 4: Duration inside

Now begin feeding meals inside the crate (bowl placed at the back). Stand near but don't close the door. If your dog will stay inside eating calmly, you're making excellent progress.

Goal: dog stays inside voluntarily for a minute or more.

## Day 5: Closing the door briefly

Start closing the door gently for 10–20 seconds while the dog eats or works on a Kong inside. Open before they show any sign of distress. If they show stress (whining, pawing at the door), you've moved too fast — go back to Day 4.

Goal: dog tolerates door closing for short durations.

## Day 6: Building duration with door closed

Practice 3–5 minute sessions with the door closed. Sit next to the crate at first. Use a filled Kong or a lick mat to create positive occupation inside. Gradually increase the duration, never going beyond what the dog can handle without distress.

Goal: 10–15 minutes calm in the crate with you present.

## Day 7: Brief absences

With the dog comfortable in the crate with you present, begin brief out-of-sight absences: leave the room for 30 seconds, return calmly (no big reunion). Gradually extend to 5–10 minutes.

Goal: dog settles in crate with you briefly out of the room.

## After the 7 days

Build from here very gradually. Many rescues become genuinely fond of their crates — a safe, predictable space in an unpredictable new environment. Never use the crate as punishment. If regression happens, go back two steps, not to the beginning.

If your dog shows severe anxiety (prolonged distress, self-injury, inability to settle), consult a certified behaviourist before continuing. Some rescue dogs have prior trauma that warrants specialist support.

title: Emergency vet bills: what to do when you can't pay
excerpt: An emergency vet visit in London can run to £3,000–£8,000. If you're facing a bill you can't meet, here are your real options.
body:
# Emergency vet bills: what to do when you can't pay

No one wants to be in this situation. But emergency veterinary bills in London routinely reach £3,000–£8,000 — and for complex cases involving surgery, imaging, and overnight hospitalisation, significantly more. If you're facing a bill you can't pay, here's what you can actually do.

## Talk to the practice first — immediately

This is the step many people skip out of embarrassment. Vet practices deal with this situation constantly. Most have a policy for it, even if it's not advertised. Options they may offer include:

- **Payment plans** — many larger practices will agree to 3–6 month payment arrangements, particularly for established clients
- **A staged treatment plan** — prioritising the most critical interventions now and scheduling others when finances allow
- **Reduction of bill** for cases of genuine financial hardship — practices have discretion, particularly charity-run practices

Be honest and be early. Calling after the treatment is done and the bill is issued is harder than raising the conversation before treatment begins.

## Veterinary charities

Several organisations provide free or subsidised veterinary treatment to owners in financial need. The qualification criteria vary.

**PDSA (People's Dispensary for Sick Animals)** — Free treatment at PDSA Pet Hospitals for pet owners receiving certain means-tested benefits (Universal Credit, Housing Benefit, etc.). There are PDSA hospitals in London; find your nearest at pdsa.org.uk.

**Blue Cross** — Operates veterinary hospitals in London (Hammersmith) for owners on qualifying benefits. Subsidised treatment available.

**RSPCA** — Some RSPCA centres provide free/subsidised treatment. The RSPCA also sometimes covers costs in cases involving cruelty or extreme hardship.

**The Cat's Protection and Dogs Trust** — May provide financial assistance for registered rehomed animals.

## Finance options

**Scratchpay and Carecredit** — US-origin products now partially available in the UK, offering instalment plans for vet bills. Acceptance varies by practice.

**Personal loan / credit card** — Not ideal, but if the alternative is refusing treatment, a 0% purchase credit card (if you can qualify) buys 12–18 months to pay without interest.

**Crowdfunding** — GoFundMe campaigns for pet medical bills have a track record of raising meaningful sums, particularly when shared via social media with a clear story and photo.

## The hardest conversation

If none of these options are viable and the prognosis is poor, a vet's job is also to be honest with you about quality of life and what treatment is realistically likely to achieve. Humane euthanasia, when the alternative is suffering, is not a failure — and a good vet will discuss this without judgment.

## The prevention: insurance

A lifetime policy for a young, healthy dog costs around £30–50/month in London. One emergency like a cruciate ligament repair (£3,000–5,000) or a foreign body ingestion (£1,500–4,000) pays for a decade of premiums. If your pet doesn't have insurance, this is the number to think about tonight.
